chapters.title: Data is a decisive argument in the fight for equality
chapters.text:
Fewer conflicts and better education share a surprising link. Both follow from greater participation by women in policy roles. The track record of female leaders is striking. When women join talks between warring countries, the chance that peace holds for at least fourteen years climbs by thirty-five percent. Portugal has raised school spending as more women entered parliament. Even with such results, many male-led fields still resist gender equality.

Men often push back when they hear about protests against discrimination, saying gender is discussed too much. They believe the world is already equal. That view is understandable, since men rarely see or hear about the daily gaps women face. Those gaps come from a data problem with two roots. First, researchers collect less data about women's lives, and we will look at why soon. Second, even when studies include women, they rarely break out the results by sex.

Why does this matter? Data shapes decisions and drives change. When Google saw that new mothers were leaving at twice the rate of other employees, the company extended paid maternity leave. Departures dropped by half. As long as half of humanity stays invisible in the numbers, their needs will keep slipping through the cracks.

Closing the data gap can lift women's health, grow GDP, and reduce crime.

Let us explore how data gaps hold societies back and survey approaches from around the world that tackle this problem. The findings are eye-opening.

chapters.title: How data-led design saves lives
chapters.text:
If you ever wish smartphones would shrink, here is a small comfort. Designers say devices will not get larger, since they have hit the size limit for the average man's hand. It is a small window into how male defaults shape the things we use every day. The same bias explains why fitness trackers underestimate calories burned during housework and why makeup can confuse VR headsets. Some of these mismatches are minor annoyances, yet others cost lives.

Most products default to male measurements.

Have you ever wondered why women face a forty-seven percent higher risk of severe injury in car crashes than men? Crash test data hints at the cause. Test dummies stand one hundred seventy-seven centimeters tall and weigh seventy-six kilograms, matching a fairly average man. That dummy misses the shape of at least half the population. Adding a female dummy is not enough. Women tend to sit more upright and closer to the pedals. The data gap led engineers to treat that posture as unusual.

A stubborn belief stops many men from seeing the blind spot in their designs. Rather than rethink the product, they blame the user. That is exactly what happened with clean cookstoves designed for low-income regions. Over seventy-five percent of households in South Asia rely on biomass such as wood or dried leaves for cooking. A single meal can take three hours or more. Women breathe fumes that rival the impact of smoking a hundred cigarettes a day, with deep costs to their health.

When aid groups handed out clean stoves, the devices gathered dust. The designers had assumed women simply lacked education. Once they collected real data, they discovered the new stoves cooked more slowly and demanded constant watching. That was not workable for women juggling many tasks. In 2015, a research team built a tool that fits into traditional stoves, boosting airflow and cutting fumes. The new design has helped families in Kenya, India, and Ghana.

Blaming users happens in wealthy countries too. One company faced criticism after its voice recognition software struggled with women's voices. Executives told users they simply needed to train more. The real cause was that the system had learned from audio recorded mostly by men.

chapters.title: The transport system is biased against women
chapters.text:
Male defaults stretch well beyond products and into city planning. Transportation planning is heavily shaped by men, so routes follow the daily commute and peak hours. London's transit map looks like a spider web that gets workers downtown fast while making every other trip awkward. But who takes those other trips.

That assumption stuck around until Spanish researchers studied women's travel patterns. They found that trips for care, such as dropping children at school or visiting a doctor, happen about as often as work commutes. Adding routes that serve those trips makes economic sense. London's hopper fare is a workable compromise, letting riders use buses and trams freely for an hour. Women running daily errands benefit the most.

Most car owners are men. In Rio de Janeiro, seventy-one percent of drivers are male.

Focusing on commutes creates another blind spot. Governments are more willing to fund new roads than repair cracked sidewalks. Walkers with strollers feel the gap every day. That is another data problem.

Snow removal reveals a similar pattern. Cities clear main arteries first, so icy conditions put many people at risk, and women are over-represented in injury reports. The Swedish city of Umeå calculated that sixty-nine percent of hospital visits in slippery weather involve women. Local authorities shifted priorities toward pedestrians and transit, and the change not only made streets safer but also saved money, since injuries and lost productivity are expensive.

Snow is not the only danger on the move. Sexual harassment hangs over daily commutes. Studies from 2016 found that ninety percent of French women had experienced harassment while traveling. Most never reported it, expecting nothing to change. The data gap is vast. Some women have even quit jobs to avoid public transit.

Fixes exist. Conductors on board help, and so do small upgrades. Because crimes happen about three times more often at stops than on board, well-lit and transparent shelters can lift safety for everyone.

chapters.title: Unpaid labor drastically affects women's income
chapters.text:
Recent research shows that working fifty hours a week hits men and women very differently. Men face lower depression risk, while women face a higher chance of serious illness. Even a forty-one to forty-five hour week can affect women's mental health. The gap is not about strength. Unpaid work, including housework and caregiving, drives the difference, and women handle seventy-five percent of that work worldwide. Health effects are only the start.

Pay and pensions take a hit. Bias is not the only reason women earn less. Many women, including forty-two percent in the UK, cut their hours to balance job and family. Caring for relatives shrinks lifetime earnings and weakens retirement benefits. Countries such as Brazil and Bolivia have introduced non-contributory pensions, a fix that helps women noticeably.

There is no such thing as a woman who doesn't work. There is only a woman who isn't paid for her work, as Caroline Criado-Perez has written.

Maternity leave policies cost some women their jobs. An Australian study showed that paid leave for at least seven months after birth helps women return to work, yet no country delivers that fully. UK women receive a third of their salary for thirty-nine weeks. Croatia, in contrast, provides thirty weeks at full pay. Some firms now offer after-school or summer programs to keep mothers on staff, though flexible hours and remote work would do even more.

Women's labor participation lifts entire economies. The World Economic Forum names it as a major driver of European growth over the past decade.

The data gap around unpaid work hides the value of social investment. Governments tend to see care infrastructure as a cost, yet the opposite is true. Investing two percent of US GDP in construction could create seven and a half million jobs, and investing in care could create thirteen million. Such investment would also let many more mothers join the workforce and lift the country's GDP.

Work brings other challenges beyond caregiving, and stories shared through the #MeToo movement show just how wide those problems run.

chapters.title: Science used to misjudge gender differences
chapters.text:
Healthcare seems like a place where data would be solid, yet textbooks tell another story. Open a chapter on the respiratory or digestive system and there is rarely a mention of sex differences. Research papers follow the same pattern. After testing drugs only on men, scientists often claim the results will help women too. The picture is shifting. Modern research shows that every organ system has sex-specific traits, and diseases often show up differently across sexes. A few examples highlight the gap.

The immune system responds more strongly in women. Vaccines can trigger stronger reactions in women, and in 2014 researchers argued that influenza vaccines should be designed with sex in mind.

Skin thickness differs. The female dermis is thinner, which means women face a higher risk of chemical exposure. Little research exists on how the mix of toxic substances in household products adds up for women.

Pain sensitivity varies. Men feel pain less sharply, so their experience became the medical norm. Doctors sometimes dismiss women's pain as emotional and prescribe antidepressants instead of real relief. Women end up consuming antidepressants at two and a half times the rate of men, even though research suggests men are more prone to depression.

Common symptoms do not always line up. The standard tuberculosis test relies on sputum, yet women rarely cough up the sample, which delays diagnosis.

Treatment effects can flip. Search online for the impact of resistance training on people with hypertension and many sites warn about increased artery stiffness. That is true for men. For women, the same exercises lower blood pressure and have a smaller effect on stiffness.

The same drug can work in opposite directions for men and women, which raises an unsettling question: how many helpful treatments have been shelved because they failed in male trials.

Some countries are tightening the rules. Federally funded trials in the United States, the European Union, and Australia now require researchers to include both sexes. The German Society of Epidemiology and the Canadian Institutes of Health Research ask researchers to factor in gender when designing studies.

Anorexia may signal autism. Since men rarely develop anorexia, researchers missed this clue for years.

chapters.title: Why women's participation in science is fundamental
chapters.text:
Gender inequality has eased compared with earlier decades, so why does the data gap remain? Psychology points to projection bias, the habit of assuming others share our habits and views.

Convincing someone a need exists is hard when they do not feel that need themselves, as Caroline Criado Perez has observed.

A field without diversity will always carry gaps, and science is a clear example. Women remain underrepresented in research worldwide. Gender bias shows up in many ways. For instance, journals accept more articles from women when reviewers cannot see the author's name, which is a worrying signal about double-blind review's absence.

Funding and citations also tilt toward men. Consider Susan Wray, whose work predicts when a cesarean is the safer choice and raised the rate of safe vaginal births from sixty-seven percent to eighty-four percent. That breakthrough matters most in regions without reliable surgical care. Yet her grant application was turned down because reviewers called the project a low priority.

Fortunately, new organizations have stepped up to support female scientists, including the foundation started by Nobel laureate Christiane Nüsslein-Volhard.

Lifting the number of women in science and backing their ideas would shrink the data gap quickly.

STEM fields, including science, technology, engineering, and math, need particular attention. Only twenty-five percent of tech workers are women, and the long-term consequences worry many observers. Artificial intelligence mirrors and amplifies the biases of its makers. What does that look like in practice.

Search results for a programmer or other male-coded jobs tend to surface men's profiles first, because the algorithm treats them as more relevant. AI hiring tools can filter women out. Some tech platforms assume strong candidates spend time on manga sites, an interest that does not match many women's habits.

Less biased models exist, and companies must choose to use them. With only eleven percent of tech executives being women, change will take time.

chapters.title: Data alone is not enough; society should act on it
chapters.text:
In 2014, BI Norwegian Business School identified five qualities of influential leaders. Women outscored men in four of them. Even so, statistics on policymakers show that gender equality is far from reality. This gap slows economic growth and keeps the data gap alive. Findings only matter once we act on them.

Politics is a key area. Studies in the United States show that female legislators push harder for women's needs and rights, yet women make up less than a quarter of politicians worldwide. Quotas work, although many people treat them with suspicion. Some argue that equality should happen naturally. The evidence says otherwise.

In 1972, the Swedish Social Democratic Party suggested that electoral lists include more women. That step and similar ones barely moved the needle. In 1993, the party introduced a zipper quota, alternating men and women on each list. After that, women's representation climbed.

Even now, a third of Sweden's female local politicians consider leaving office, since threats make the job unsustainable. Data confirms that this pattern repeats in many countries.

The fact that ninety-three percent of venture capitalists are men is bad news for women entrepreneurs. Venture capital drives tech startups, so this imbalance narrows support for women's projects. When Debbie Woskow co-founded AllBright to fund female innovators, her male colleagues dismissed the venture as charity. They were surprised to hear she planned to make money from it.

Boston Consulting Group found that women-led startups receive over one million dollars less in funding than men-led ones, yet their revenues run higher.

Disaster response is another front. Women suffer more in natural disasters, partly because of men's violence and gender inequality. In Sri Lanka, for example, parents rarely teach girls to swim or climb trees, treating those skills as male. Floods hit women harder as a result. Refugee camp planning benefits from women's input. The simple absence of separate bathrooms, a detail male planners often miss, raises the risk of violence against women.

In Bangladesh, women cannot go out alone and must travel with a male relative, which costs them precious minutes during evacuations.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin discovered that stars are mostly hydrogen and helium, yet most textbooks never mention her name. Cave paintings in France and Spain often go unattributed to the women who created them. Many Americans know their country has not won the men's World Cup, but few realize that the women's national team has won it four times. These oversights are not accidents. They come from a culture shaped by the male perspective.

Many parts of life, from devices to public spaces, were designed by men and for men. New efforts are changing that picture. Sweden introduced a month of parental leave that fathers must use or lose, and uptake rose sharply. South Korea and Iceland have adopted the same idea. In India, activists built a platform that flags unsafe districts and helps women pick safer transit routes. Several tech firms now use blind hiring systems, and they have hired more employees from non-traditional backgrounds as a result.

Initiatives differ, yet most aim to close the representation gap. One person's success can lift many others. Women scientists tend to study issues that affect women, and women journalists are more likely to cover those findings.

Change starts with the recognition that doing nothing keeps the imbalance in place. Everyone shares the same boat, and attention to others' needs helps us all.

Watch TED Talks by women leaders and notice how they make change every day. Read a book about minorities to widen your view. Look up women's participation in your country's politics.
